original_abstract: One of the most successful missionary organization, the Jesuits, also operated in northern Mexico in the area today identified as the Sonoran Desert, comprising the Mexican state of Sonora and the US state of Arizona. By the early eighteenth century increasingly German-speaking missionaries arrived and worked hard in the wide network of missions originally established by the pioneer Padre Eusebio Kino (d. 1711) to spread God's words. They also worked intensively on studying that territory and produced numerous written records about their experiences, their research, and the conditions of that world. They were expelled in 1767 and banned globally in 1783, but until then they achieved greatest success with the native population. Their missionary accounts prove to be highly exiting historical, anthropological, religious, and scientific documents most important for missionary history.
humanized_abstract: The Sonoran Desert region of northern Mexico in what is now the state of Sonora and the U.S. state of Arizona was also part of Jesuit missionary activities. It was in this region that, in the early eighteenth century, large numbers of German-speaking missionaries entered the extensive system of missions that had been set up by the pioneering Catholic missionary, Padre Eusebio Kino (d. 1711) to bring people to the Gospel. The Jesuits also did a huge amount of research on the region, leaving behind many valuable accounts of their missionary and scientific work. They were expelled from Mexico in 1767 and banned worldwide in 1783 but, while they existed, they were arguably the most successful of the missionaries among the indigenous peoples of Mexico. The Jesuit missionary accounts are, for historians, anthropologists, theologians and scientists, an unparalleled rich and fascinating source of historical, anthropological, theological and scientific knowledge, unparalleled in missionary history.

The Jesuits also did a huge amount of research on the region, leaving behind many valuable accounts of their missionary and scientific work. They were expelled from Mexico in 1767 and banned worldwide in 1783 but, while they existed, they were arguably the most successful of the missionaries among the indigenous peoples of Mexico. The Jesuit missionary accounts are, for historians, anthropologists, theologians and scientists, an unparalleled rich and fascinating source of historical, anthropological, theological and scientific knowledge, unparalleled in missionary history.
